speaker: interviewer
text: December 1st, 1996. We are interviewing Lillian Bielsky-Bell. The interviewer is Bonnie Gurewitsch. We are in Brooklyn, New York in the United States, and we are speaking English. December 1st, 1996. I'm interviewing Lillian Bielsky-Bell. My name is Bonnie Gurewitsch. We are in Brooklyn, New York in the United States, and we are speaking English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. Bielsky, can you tell us your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Lillian Bielsky-Bell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-I-E-L-S-K-I-- Y B-E-L-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ticktin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: T-I-C-K-T-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 19th, 20-- January 13, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Bialystok for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-I-A-L-Y-S-T-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yesha-- Yeshayahu.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ticktin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that his only name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father was very sick as he was young. And a name was added to his name-- Alter. It means he should get older and get out of the sickness. This was a superstition from the old home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which of his names was used more frequently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alter. You will be surprised.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zina.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was handling, you know, so-called rags, called schmattas, which it was turned in-- in-- into paper in big factories in the big cities, like Grodno, Bialystok, and Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what exactly did he do with the rags?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had people that he sold-- he didn't sell to them, actually. They traded stuff that the peasant didn't had in the villages, that they couldn't get to it. So he used to have, like soap, and dishes, and all kind of necessary things that the peasant needed for their farms, for their-- for to go on with their lives and couldn't afford to buy it in the cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used to trade with them. And for that, he used to give the people schmattas, which the people took this from my-- from my father. And they collected the schmattas. And we had big, big warehouses. And they used to bring there-- their work people-- they put it, like, in-- in big stacks, you know, separate stacks. And they-- they used to ship this to the big cities to turn to paper. Was that a comfortable living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very comfortable. You know, at that time, in our small towns, Daddy was really a capitalist. They looked at him. He-- he was-- he was making a nice living. He had a lot of money. But my father never lived for himself. He always lived for others.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how about your mother? Did she work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was a lady of-- she could afford-- my father made a very nice living. And, you know, she took care on her kids. She was from the old-- from the old timers. Loving ballet, loving opera, loving shows, speaking beautiful Russian-- you know, from the old, old intellectuals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just had only one brother. We were two kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was six years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you remember him? What was his personality?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember him that, when he had to come for vacations home, I used to kiss a week ahead, the chair where-- the chairs were assigned. Here was Daddy sitting, here was mommy sitting, my chair, and his chair. So his chair, I used always to kiss a week before because I knew that soon, soon he will come and he will be sitting on it. We had a very close relationship, period. Our family was a very, very close and devoted family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he come home from? Where he-- had he gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: He finished Tarbut in our little shtetl [INAUDIBLE], where we lived-- where Daddy moved when I was, I think, nine months old or a year. And then, when he finished Tarbut, they sent him to Bialystok to the high school, to the Hebrew high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which at that time, it was a very expensive proposition. It was on the same level like here, for instance, college. And not everybody could afford that. It was a private thing. He lived on my-- the whole family was in Bialystok-- my father's sisters. So he lived by one of my father's sisters. Her name was Rifka. And from there, he used to go-- he finished the whole-- he finished-- there was eight classes and two lyceums. Lyceum is, you know, like advanced two years of college.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attend the same Tarbut school-- six classes. Six or eight, I don't remember exactly. Already what my brother did. Because my father supported the Tarbut. He was the one on the committee. He was the one that-- kids that could not afford, or didn't have any, let's say, any book, or any notes to-- notebook to buy-- it was everything from my father's hands. Whoever had to be married, whoever died and didn't have for the funeral, [INAUDIBLE] whoever had to get married and didn't have a penny for a wedding. My mother used to buy all-- all the colors, the trousseaus, and then when a child was born, she had to make the bris. You know, she was-- we-- anybody that lived in that shtetl knew that, if they needed something, he was like the father of the-- of the shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father involved in the Kehillah? Was he an officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was always a-- a Kehillah. He was always in-- in all the committees, you know, took part. He was a member of the shul. The holidays, were always-- we always had company. We always had people coming to us. Friday especially, was a very hard day, because, you know, usually he paid them always matzah shabbas-- the-- the-- whatever the pay belonged to them. But people, they did not have money to make shabbas. He-- they used to come and he paid them Friday, to buy a piece of challah, to buy this, or to buy chicken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a traditional household?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish traditional house. It wasn't very, extremely, extremely Hasidic, but it was a traditional household.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember in particular one of the holidays that you could describe to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I didn't like the holidays, because I-- I didn't like the food of the holidays. I was a very poor eater. So-- but the holidays, my mother forced me for the chicken soup, with the [INAUDIBLE], with all that. This was the biggest tragedy. Any new clothes that I didn't like it-- they didn't buy never clothes for me in the shtetl. Daddy traveled a lot. He used to go to the big cities like Bialystok, Vilna, Warsaw, you know, for business. So always he came full-handed with all kind of clothing, with [INAUDIBLE] coats and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and stuff that you didn't see. The whole shtetl played with my toys. Even when I wasn't home, and they used to come around the window to look if I'm home. Mommy always let them in and she said, she's not home. She'll come soon, but you can play with her dolls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because my dolls-- still I hear people from my shtetl that I talk very little about, but they tell everybody the story when they shocked me-- when they shocked me and-- by telling. I said, how do you know? They said, why? Do you think there's no witnesses? They played with your dolls. Always-- they were always welcome. It was a open house for everybody who needed it. Did you mind that they came in and played with your things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Because Mom was always ahead of me. She said, if you have it, you have to share. But not everybody could afford it. I had dolls from the world, you know, talking dolls. At that time, it was-- everything was a novelty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there one doll that was your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- it was a favorite doll. You would be surprised that I had the doll even after the war, and I lost the doll in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you keep that doll?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I found him-- I found it after the war by a peasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that doll? Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a blonde big doll that my aunt sent me for a birthday, or a different occasion. I don't remember what was the occasion. But Daddy came and he schlepped her like a baby. And I said, what's that? They said, that is your present that the aunt sent you from Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember really. Because each one that played with her had a favorite name, and they named her each time a different name. But I had a special corner in the house where it was a table with the dolls, with the toys, with the kitchen, with the-- you know, it was something that really nobody had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I always thought about myself-- especially now when I have my own grandchildren already-- how nice it was of my mother at that time, you know? That time, nobody was thinking about war. Nobody-- it was a free world. But always her understanding, you have and the other one doesn't have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she always took this under consideration and said to me, you have to share. God didn't give only to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born? What-- what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1926. January 13th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about the Tarbut school? What was the curriculum like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Tarbut school-- I wasn't a bad-- a bad student. But I was sometimes a lazy student. And I had to be reminded. It's the truth. Languages, I was perfect. Perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which languages did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was Polish. At home, when they didn't want me to understand-- I told you, Mother came from a very exceptional fine home. And when they didn't want me to understand this, [INAUDIBLE] Russian. I understood every word, but I couldn't speak. During the war, I started to speak because I heard more and more and I went to a Russian school. Now-- and I forgot 50 years later. Now when the Russian immigrants came, everything came to me fluently. And-- and even better than I-- I knew before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn at the Tarbut school? What languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Hebrew-- math, physics, history, and geography.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much of that was taught in Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was taught in Hebrew. Polish was just a language, only an hour or two in the higher classes. In the lower classes, it was practically only Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to speak Hebrew fluently?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke Hebrew fluently. My teachers are still in Israel. I still have letters from them. He's very devoted. He always, when it comes to my husband's memorial, he always makes a speech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ickowicz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He's a very sick man, but with his last-- I-- really he's making a big big effort to participate in the memorials. And if I'm there or not, the memorial is going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the Tarbut school, which school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Tarbut school, the-- my brother finished, in '38, the high school. And he was about to leave for-- for higher education to London, because my father had a brother there and he got married in London. And they wanted him to come there and study in England. And I was supposed to go in his place to the same aunt, to the same room, and to attend the high school, which it was like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came back for vacation-- for the winter vacation. Right away, we were the second ones in our shtetl that we had a phone and a radio. That was a big deal in '38. The druggist was one, and my father, with the business he had in the warehouses in his office, and he had home. And we had a connection from home to the-- to the warehouses. And, you know, I would come home at Hanukkah and the latkes and all the goodies, and I was very lazy to do my homework. And all of a sudden, I see the time is moving and I have to go back. And it won't help me, and I have to do what I have to do. And as I sat down, my father was by neighbor, so that I had to open the door, you know. And he was playing [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother went to visit, to pay a shiva call, to this druggist that he was very good friends with them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a tragical story there, and that's why-- and my mother was so involved in it, because the mo-- they had a only daughter, and the daughter married somebody they didn't approve of it. And mother told her, if you marry him, no matter how I'm shivaing over you, will find me dead. Because she wasn't a wealthy-- a healthy woman. And so it was. She married and mother died. And my mother went to pay the druggist a shiva call. And I was sitting home and doing-- took out my books and the phone rang. I knew that nobody would call me, because there weren't any phones besides the druggist. Sure enough, it was the druggist called me, saying come fast for the doctor. Mommy's not feeling well, and it's very bad. As she walked into the house-- you know, you don't say anything when you walk into a shiva house-- she sat down on the step-- on the chair. The druggist was sitting the shiva, and she fell off the chair and that was it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was on my birthday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, I called right away my father. I will never forget, our doctor-- which it was a doctor, not a specialist, like today for every little thing is a specialist. But this was a doctor for everything-- for head and feet and hands and everything. But they lived on a lot of steps, and I couldn't walk up the steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: I called my father. He ran to the drugstore. And I wanted to call the-- the doctor to come because, you know, a little shtetl, and my father was so famous. So I knew that she'll run. And-- but I couldn't go on the steps. I climbed. I-- I-- you know, I crawled. I could not make one step.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally we got there and it was too late. As she walked out, as she was dressed, with her lipstick and with everything. You would never say-- in fact, when we came home and he wanted to put her on the floor, I didn't let it because I said, look she's warm. She's-- she's alive. What are you doing to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Help her. Help her. But don't-- don't put her on the floor because she's not dead. Unfortunately, that's what had happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my life started. This was in 1938. My mother was 47 years old. And that's when my Holo-- originally, my Holocaust started.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- that was my 13th birthday. You know, we had a very nice relationship. They shivaed over me. They-- they-- they-- one girl and one boy, you know, and they wanted to raise us in the right way, with the traditional home, with the discipline, with the kavod.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, a-- a sort of shock. I personally never knew my mother's name, because they had such a name between them, between father and mother, that I never knew that my mother has a real name until-- you know, like, called Zina. I never knew because they had such a-- words between them-- such a-- no, what can I tell you? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Favorites between them that I never knew who was called what. What did your father call your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Russian names, all kind, you know, like baby, like sweetheart, like-- I never heard her name called by my father, until I asked one time. I said, Mommy, do you have a name? She said, what's the matter with you? So she told me the story. There was a real very fantastic life. But this was absolutely nothing. My father got very sick. He couldn't-- he couldn't cope with it. He smoked a lot. He stopped eating. His whole intestines were shot. And the doctors decided-- we took him to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Vilna to a doctor. Then we took them to Bialystok and the decision was that he has to go to a convalescent home where they start feeding him by spoon by spoon, you know. And he was seven months in a convalescent home. And from all over, I remained alone from all the luxury, from everything. My brother was with me. My-- my father's brother came, you know, and my mother's brother came. So they stayed. They took turns to stay with the-- with the kids. I had to go back to school. It was not easy after such a tragedy. And Mommy used to go away for a week to see my brother in Bialystok. It was a tragedy. I was crying. I was screaming. I didn't sleep. I didn't eat. Mommy, Mommy, Mommy. And here Mommy was taking forever. And I just couldn't get to the idea. And here knowing maybe I lose my father too. It was a terrible time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I-- My brother took me back to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I had to finish out the year. Come home for the summer. Before the sum-- before the vacation, he calls me. He says to me, you know, Daddy and I decided that I don't want you to travel alone. I'll come to pick you up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why? I'm already a big girl. And I can travel myself. It wasn't easy, because everything was by train. You know, there wasn't any planes from city to city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no no no. I'm coming to pick you up. I-- I don't trust you. All right. He came before, and he spent with me a week in Bialystok. We went here. We went there. We went to visit grandpa and grandma. We stayed with them a little bit. And finally we had to go home. As-- and I was very upset. You know, I felt so alone. I was shy to tell somebody-- my brother, I was very, very close with. But as a little girl, I-- there were such a woman things that I wasn't-- I was shy to share with him. My father wouldn't ask because he wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- who did I remain with? My housekeeper. My mother always prepared me about womanhood, about everything. But details of life, she never told me because I didn't get to it. And here I wanted to ask, and I felt so bad to come to my housekeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: The housekeeper, in fact, they brought-- when they came to the-- to the shtetl to live and to settle down, they brought her from Bialystok. And she was, until my-- my-- and she was with us until my brother went to Bialystok, to-- to high school. And always, when-- when they want to scold us or punish us, or-- she said, first punish me. First hit me. First do everything to me before you do anything to my kids. She called us my kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perla. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then she left. She got old, and she left. So we got somebody new. New. In my time, she was still about 10, 15 years with us. But I felt so embarrassed. I said, what's the matter with me? How could God punish me so badly not to have a soul in the world to share my feelings? Anyway, my-- I'm going back to the story when my brother took me home. Just when we came to Maladzyechna, because this was our station. From there, we had to take-- we had a special man that used to pick us up and bring us to the station. It was quite a few kilomet-- kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we stopped with the train in Maladzyechna, my brother said to me, I have to tell you something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, right away it came on my-- on my mind that maybe something happened to Daddy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said, what happened to Daddy? He says, nothing. Nothing to Daddy. I said, you're hiding something from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said-- you know, he was thinking and thinking. He said, yes. I'm hiding from you something. Daddy is home. He recovered. He feels fine. On his way home from the convalescent home, he met a woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: That he swore that never in his life, nobody can substitute with my mother. This was his friend. This was his everything, his mother from his children. No-- nobody can substitute with her. And here he has very serious thoughts. I said, you want to tell me that Daddy's getting married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you said it. I didn't put out this from my mouth. But it looks like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- and we have to stop and see her because Daddy's there and he's waiting for us. She lived in Maladzyechna. She was born in Vilna. She had two sisters and a mother. And she had-- her husband was a actor. They divorced and she remained with the child-- with a boy that was a very intelligent, extremely intelligent, very good looking. And she raised him. His name, his-- his [INAUDIBLE] was Weron-- Weronski, her husband's name. But they-- after they divorced, she had nothing to do and she had to-- he went to the [INAUDIBLE]. He was an engineer. That's what he drived to. I don't know if completely he finished. Maybe the war interfered with him. But she had started to-- alterations. She learned alterations, and that's what she-- how she raised him. And she had to-- to live on something. Here she saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here she saw a very, very, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very rich man, very well dressed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she thought, that's a good catch. [SIGHS] Intelligent she was, very bright, very smart. To make the story short, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] she caught him, and they married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For me, it was a tragedy. First of all, she was so involved with her son that she thought that he's the only one in the world and there is nobody like him. And sure enough, he had the preferences. And the minute she walked into the house, my Holocaust started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- the only thing is that I wasn't too much home in the beginning because I still was in Bialystok in school. It was in '39 when I came home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they were already married. She was the-- the boss of the house, the [INAUDIBLE]. After every-- Mommy left everything in gold and in silver and everything, my heart busted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you present at the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Daddy didn't tell nobody. That was the reason that my brother came to pick me up. I shouldn't get a shock coming into the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came home, had they already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were already married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't know if they were married, to be honest with you. But I know-- they married later on, but I know they were together. I know that my-- the-- the bedroom of my father and mother wasn't anymore my father's. It was a mutual bed room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was very hard for me. You know, I don't know what she wanted to prove, but my father suffered terribly. He was between the hammer-- his daughter and his wife. I guess he liked her. She was extremely intelligent person. Any book, any-- any film, any-- any movie-- everything, you could talk with her. And she had-- and then, you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when-- in '39, when they started-- already when the Russian came in and the Poles had to leave-- and my father was a capitalist and a very, very rich man. And they wanted him. They want everything, and they wanted a [INAUDIBLE]-- they took away everything from him. And, you know, like the business-- he wasn't any boss anymore. But him, personally, and his family, they wanted to ship to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have to stop now to change the tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Already?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll start again.
